ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a modestly novel architectural tweak (key-value and key-value-predict splits of the LSTM output; §§2.2–2.3) and an empirical finding that attention mainly uses the ∼5 most recent outputs, motivating a simple N‑gram RNN (§2.4, contributions i–iii). However, the idea of separating keys/values is explicitly inspired by prior work (Miller et al., Ba et al., Reed & de Freitas, Gulcehre et al.) and primarily constitutes an application/extension to language modeling rather than a fundamentally new mechanism. Overall this is an incremental but useful contribution that clearly differentiates itself from prior models and provides informative empirical insight.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow (Introduction → Methods with subsections 2.1–2.4 → Related Work → Experiments → Results → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (end of §1 lists the three contributions). The notation is largely consistent and well-defined (numbered equations 1–13 and clearly described key/value/predict splits), and the manuscript regularly references figures and results to support claims. Presentation issues are minor but noticeable: there are several duplicated/garbled sentences and repeated citation fragments (e.g., multiple repeated citation sequences and duplicated paragraphs in the provided text), occasional typos (e.g., “probablity”), and some references to figures/tables whose clarity cannot be judged here — these reduce polish but do not obscure the core exposition.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (key-value(-predict) attention improves perplexity; attentive LMs mainly use a short ~5-token history; a simple N-gram RNN matches more complex models) and presents targeted experiments (Wikipedia corpus and CBT) that support them, including attention-window sweeps and attention-position analyses. The mathematical formulations of the attention, key-value and key-value-predict splits are consistent and correct for the described architectures. Experimental methodology is generally sound (appropriate baselines including RMN and HORNN, matching parameter counts, validation-based model selection, training details), but the paper lacks statistical significance testing and depends on reimplementations (e.g. RM(+tM-g)) without verifiable reproduction details, leaving small uncertainty about comparative claims. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only minor gaps (no significance tests, limited ablation to fully isolate causes).
